sources.title: The Bigamist
sources.description: Taken form IMDB : Harry and Eve Graham are trying to adopt a baby. The head of the agency senses Harry is keeping a secret and does some investigating. He soon discovers Harry has done an unusual amount of traveling from his home in San Francisco to Los Angeles. Harry gets tracked down in LA where he has a second wife and a baby. Via flashbacks, Harry tells the adoption agent how he ended up in two marriages.

sources.title: The Bigamist (1953) | Colorized Public Domain Classic Drama with Joan Fontaine & Edmond O’Brien
sources.description:
The Bigamist (1953) is a gripping American drama directed by and starring Ida Lupino, making it one of the earliest Hollywood films to tackle the taboo subject of bigamy with depth and nuance. This restored and colorized edition breathes new life into a thought-provoking story that blends mid-century melodrama with social commentary. Originally released in stark black and white, our enhanced color version offers modern audiences in the USA, Europe, and worldwide a more immersive experience, especially for those watching on large screens and televisions.

Plot Summary:
Harry Graham (Edmond O’Brien) appears to be a successful businessman living a comfortable life with his wife, Eve (Joan Fontaine). On the surface, their marriage seems stable, and together they hope to adopt a child. When Mr. Jordan (Edmund Gwenn), the head of the adoption agency, investigates their background, he uncovers a shocking secret: Harry is already married to another woman in a different city.

Through flashbacks, the story reveals Harry’s loneliness, the emotional distance between him and Eve, and his fateful meeting with Phyllis (Ida Lupino), a warm and compassionate woman who offers him the affection and companionship he craves. Struggling between two women and two lives, Harry becomes trapped in a moral and legal dilemma that ultimately exposes the fragile realities of love, marriage, and human weakness. The climax forces Harry to confront the law and both women, leaving audiences to reflect on the complexities of fidelity, duty, and compassion.

Cast and Crew:
• Director: Ida Lupino
• Producer: Collier Young
• Screenplay: Collier Young
• Cinematography: George E. Diskant
• Editing: Stanford Tischler
Starring:
• Joan Fontaine as Eve Graham
• Edmond O’Brien as Harry Graham
• Ida Lupino as Phyllis Martin
• Edmund Gwenn as Mr. Jordan

Film Significance:
The Bigamist is historically significant for several reasons. Ida Lupino, already recognized as a pioneering actress and director, broke new ground as the only woman directing feature films in 1950s Hollywood. By taking on a socially sensitive issue like bigamy, the film avoided sensationalism and instead offered a sympathetic, humanistic perspective on flawed individuals caught in difficult circumstances. Joan Fontaine’s performance as the refined but emotionally distant wife contrasts sharply with Lupino’s portrayal of a warm and vulnerable single woman, highlighting the conflicting needs that drive Harry’s choices.

The film also resonates as a cultural artifact of postwar America, exploring themes of loneliness, societal expectations, and the hidden struggles beneath seemingly “perfect” suburban lives. Its restrained, realistic style distinguished it from more melodramatic Hollywood productions of the era, making it a favorite among cinephiles and scholars today.

Enhanced Public Domain Explanation:
This film is in the public domain, which means it is completely free of copyright restrictions and can be legally shared, viewed, studied, and preserved without limitation. Colorized Public Domain restores and enhances these historic works through careful digital cleaning, visual restoration, and colorization techniques. Our mission is to ensure that timeless classics like The Bigamist remain available to a worldwide audience. By presenting them in color and modern quality, we make them more engaging and accessible to younger generations who might otherwise overlook black-and-white cinema. This process respects the original film while providing a new layer of appreciation for today’s viewers.

Why Watch This Colorized Edition:
The original 1953 release was in black and white, typical of its modest budget and serious tone. While historically accurate, modern audiences—especially in the USA and Europe—are more likely to engage with colorized restorations. By presenting the film in color, we highlight the emotional contrasts between Harry’s two worlds, making the drama more accessible and visually appealing without diminishing its original impact.

Subscribe and Explore More Classics:
📌 Subscribe for more colorized classics: https://www.youtube.com/@ColorizedPublicDomain?sub_confirmation=1

We upload restored and colorized films from the Golden Age of cinema, from forgotten B-movies to culturally significant classics. Our work ensures these treasures remain discoverable, enjoyable, and preserved for audiences in the USA, Europe, and worldwide.

sources.title: Bigamist, The
sources.description: You can find more information regarding this film on its IMDb page .

sources.title: The Bigamist (1953) IDA LUPINO
sources.description:
Director: Ida Lupino
Stars: Joan Fontaine, Ida Lupino, Edmund Gwenn

A couple, having grown apart after they are unable to conceive a child of their own, decides to adopt. But things take a troubling turn when the adoption agency conducts a background check and discovers the husband leading a double life with another family in L.A.

sources.title: The Bigamist 1953 ★★★½ CC: Drama/Film-Noir [1:19:31] Joan Fontaine , Edmond O'Brien, Ida Lupino
sources.description: The Bigamist is a 1953 American drama film noir directed by Ida Lupino starring Joan Fontaine, Ida Lupino, Edmund Gwenn and Edmond O'Brien. Producer/Screenwriter Collier Young was married to Fontaine at the time and had previously been married to Lupino. The Bigamist has been cited as the first American feature film made in the sound era in which the female star of a film directed herself. San Francisco businessman Harry Graham (Edmond O'Brien) and his wife and business partner, Eve (Joan Fontaine), are in the process of adopting a child. When private investigator Jordan (Edmund Gwenn) uncovers the fact that Graham has another wife, Phyllis (Ida Lupino), and a small child in Los Angeles, he confesses everything. In flashback, Graham tells of the strains in his marriage with Eve and how he found himself falling for the kind-hearted waitress Phyllis while on a business trip. Cast: Joan Fontaine as Eve Graham Ida Lupino as Phyllis Martin Edmund Gwenn as Mr. Jordan Edmond O'Brien as Harry Graham / Harrison Graham Kenneth Tobey as Tom Morgan, Defense Attorney Jane Darwell as Mrs. Connelley Peggy Maley as Phone Operator Lilian Fontaine as Miss Higgins, Landlady Soundtracks: It Wasn't the Stars That Thrilled Me Written by Matt Dennis and Dave Gillam Performed by Matt Dennis (uncredited)

He soon discovers Harry has done an unusual amount of traveling from his home in San Francisco to Los Angeles. Harry gets tracked down in LA where he has a second wife and a baby. Via flashbacks, Harry tells the adoption agent how he ended up in two marriages.
